speaker: interviewer
text: --five. Interview with Sol Urbach. Take number six. We were talking about your life in the United States, but to go back to Bamberg for a little bit, what about your social life? I mean, you know, you were a young man.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. When I arrived in, in Germany as a displaced person in 1949, as I explained, we did receive a, a cold welcome from the German population, and we received an apartment, where we formed a unit of four friends. We began to socialize. The, the-- we had the good fortune in those days that the GIs that just came through the war and had already an understanding of what the Germans did to this-- to our people were extremely friendly. They would go out of their way to receive us-- to receive us and help us, and socialize with us. And, and it was fairly common that we would meet with some GIs, particularly of Jewish faith, and they-- we would become drinking buddies of coke and, and some, some homemade whiskies. And we socialized, and we were together for Jewish holidays and so on. We also had a-- I had a circle of friends, of, of female and male friends. The, the spirits began to wake up, and we began to lead a normal life after-- if that's what you can call it. It, it was not-- the, the memories of what happened to us were not far from our mind, but yet life has its way to begin to shape. And we did go to-- we socialized, and we went to dances, and we had enough food. The UNRRA began to function and distribute food, so we did have enough food. We began-- I also began to work and keep a job so I would have some money. So life began to return to normal. In 1946, still in Bamberg, a group of girls-- if I recall it, three young ladies-- arrived from a school in Frankfurt in 1946. It would be-- it would have been in November of 19-- November or December of 1946. This group of three ladies arrived in Bamberg. And upon arrival in Bamberg, they figured out quickly where the bachelors are and where the few bachelors lived together in one apartment, and that was us. And the three ladies came to visit these three bachelors with the intention that we would go to a dance. Among these three ladies was a young lady who was beautiful, attractive, and she seemed to have thrown the spell on me immediately. Because when I met these three ladies-- this was my future wife. Ada Birnbaum at that time-- but when she arrived in Bamberg, I was telling her how I desperately needed to go to Munich tomorrow. I got to get to the railroad station, and I'll be gone in the morning. And I would not return. And she just reminded me of that incident, but she was-- her ability to work the spell, worked it out that I never did go to Munich. I remained, remained there in Bamberg. And we continued our courtship from then on. From 1946 we kept company all the way through to 1949. In 1949, I was-- I had to leave Germany because I was granted permission to come to this country, and the Birnbaum family was not granted permission yet to leave Germany and come here. So I came here alone, and my cousins by the name of Posner-- it's a daughter of the Baldingers-- welcomed me in her home. Frida and Bill Posner, and I lived with them and began to work. Sometimes three months later or so, Ada Birnbaum arrived in this country by herself because the papers were not completed for the whole family to arrive. She was the only one that could leave. So she left, and I was here waiting for her when she arrived on General McRae ship in New York, and, again, from 1946, it was the interruption of that three-months span we kept company. We continued our courtship until 19-- 1950. And I was saying that I arrived in March 13, 19-- 1949. And Ada arrived here in June of 1949. And we remained-- we kept company until 1950. And in 1950, we got married in New York City. In New York City, we-- I was working as a furniture maker. I was working at the time in Paterson, living in Paterson. It was in my cousin's before I was married. But after we were married I lived in New York together with Ada, and I traveled to Paterson to a job. The community at large was not extremely helpful. Even the Jewish organizations were hardly functioning to really put you back on your feet and guide you well enough. I was very much on my own other than receiving assistance from my family, who permitted me to stay with them. I was very much alone. In 1950, when we got married, we stayed in New York for a while, and in 1951, my in-laws Joe and Anne Birnbaum and their family-- and Ada was one of the three children-- moved to Flemington, New Jersey, to run a poultry farm. When they moved to Flemington, New Jersey, I decided-- we both decided, I should say-- we both decided that our best bet would be to move to the small town of Flemington. Also, we did not know how you get a job here, and because I was already a union member as a carpenter in New York whether I would be able to transfer my union card to here. I didn't know, but nevertheless we decided to move to Flemington, New Jersey. Before I even came to Flemington, when I just arrived in this country, my Uncle Baldinger, from Paterson, his first duty was to take this new displaced person that just arrived to the selective service and report me that I have arrived in this country, and I could be of Army age. And I was classified 1A, but I was never called to the service because shortly thereafter I was married, and shortly thereafter Ada was expecting a child. And in those days when they drafted people for the Korean War, they did not draft people that were married and had children already. First they drafted the married men without children, but later on they upped it to if you had a child, and we had a child. Our oldest son was born by that time-- our oldest son David-- so I never served in the Army in the United States. Came to Flemington In 1951. Work was extremely hard to get, but I did find some work as a carpenter. That was the only thing I could do, but in 19-- 1953, I found a young man in Flemington, New Jersey, that was equally talented with me in the trade of carpentry, and we decided to throw our lots together and become partners. We were partners with the intention to do any work that comes up-- repair roofs, repair chicken houses, dig foundations, whatever would come up our way. The two of us were strong, young. We were-- we were just 25, 26 years old. We would take on anything that came our way, and we formed a business. That business was formed. It was a-- me supplying the capital. I had $130. This was the capital for the business. And my partner had a shovel, pick, a wheelbarrow, and a transit level. And those items were worth $130. Together with my capital investment we became partners. And we began to function as a business, building business, for the next six years, as long as we remained partners. And after the six years were up, we were both well off. We had the finances. $130 turned into hundreds of thousands of dollars of-- worth of properties that we possessed at that time. And we parted, and from then on I went into business myself and operated a prosperous business until my retirement. Upon my retirement, I was able to sell a complex of apartments worth $3.8 million. And I was also selling properties worth many hundreds of thousands of dollars. So the hard work and the investment of $130 worked out very well. We became respected citizens in the community. We worked on many fund drives for many different things. We became involved with the Jewish community of this small town of Flemington. We raised a family here. Our oldest son was born in New York, but he was a Flemingtonian at that time. He was born here. Then our daughter was born here and our youngest sons. They were all born in Flemington, attended schools in Flemington, and they did fairly well for themselves. Our oldest son is now a cardiologist, and he's on Cape Cod. Our middle daughter is an attorney, and she works in New York City and in, in New Jersey. And our youngest son graduating from Princeton University, graduating then from Columbia School in architecture, is now a PhD candidate in Princeton again at this moment. We have five grandchildren. The grandchildren are Rebecca, who is 14, Simone, that is 12, Jonathan, that is seven. Samuel is nine years old, and Elise is seven years old. These are our five grandchildren. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if I may look back-- if I could get that, that album of mine--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or do we want-- why don't we do that when we do the pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, if you want to ask some questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. [INAUDIBLE] Just when the movie came out, or when you heard about it, what was your reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: My reaction of and the making was a unbelievable inner upheaval really. I just didn't know how to deal with it, how a film will be able to deal with a story of-- that complicated kind of a story. I was so tremendously anxious. I couldn't figure it out, how anybody could put together a picture with that complicated of a story. When the movie came out finally, I discovered that it will be shown in Montreal, Canada. And I had to get to see it first, because I was so anxious to see it. We traveled up to-- got in the car and traveled up to Montreal to get on line and see the movie first. The-- when the movie opens up-- when opens up, it sort of sets you back in your seat. I was there with Ada. We held hands for the-- for the duration of the movie. We-- It sort of sets you back that you begin to feel that you're back in the trenches of, of the concentration camp. It has just the enormous power of opening up and making you feel that this is real. It's just a-- At least, that's the kind of impact it had on me. And this was early on. This was December of 1993. And my opinion of the movie was that it is a absolute winner of describing what the Holocaust looked like that somehow the public will be able to grasp it better than they were able to grasp the movies that were made up to now, where the big transports went to conc-- to Auschwitz, and people didn't come out, and millions of people were lost and all that-- that this one had somewhat of a personal ability to reach every person so that he would understand. And when I-- upon reviewing this movie, I saw that he was not even showing extreme brutalities in this movie, and yet, he accomplished that. So I, I knew immediately in 1993 that this would be recognized by the public as a great film. My own feeling was it was great, and the public would probably recognize it. I just felt so good about it, that, that it would be a renewal of the-- of the learning about the Holocaust. I, I felt it instantly, that that would be the effect this movie would have. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The other part why I thought that this movie will be seen by a lot of people is because I somehow felt that, that a lot of people will want to go to the movie and see what a good German looked like. And this would have another effect of a lot of people wanting to see the movie. And later on, we learned that that's exactly how it was. And it did have the effect on people on wanting to see and recognizing the movie as one of the great Holocaust movies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see Oskar Schindler after May 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did see Oskar Schindler. Immediately upon my arrival in-- still in 1945 in Germany, I discovered that Oskar Schindler is in Munich, and I would occasionally travel from, from-- that is, after 1949, when I arrived in Germany. I don't know whether I said '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: '49 or '46 when you were in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm sorry. '45. I said it correctly first.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I arrived in Germany in 1945, I heard that Oskar Schindler is in Munich, and I made trips to Munich to see him as often as I could. In 19-- in 1974, I was in Israel on a trip with my family, and I discovered that Oskar Schindler is visiting there. And I went to see Oskar Schindler in 1974. I saw him in his hotel in Israel. He was there with one of his lady friends, a new one that I did not know. And he was after a mild stroke, but nevertheless, he did recognize me. And we chatted for a while. It was fairly obvious that a long chat would not be in his best interest because he was not well. And so we had a short chat, but nevertheless it turned out to be a very important one because this was the year that Oskar Schindler died in. And this was the same year that my family saw him in Israel. Just recently we went on a trip together just this past September, 1995. My family-- Ada, myself, with three children, a daughter-in-law, a son-in-law, and a-- five grandchildren, making 12 of us-- undertook a trip to-- back to Poland and to Israel on a sort of educational and inspirational kind of a trip for the family to the learn, and to study again, and to learn about what the Holocaust was like. So we went to stomping cemeteries in Poland. We went to Auschwitz. We did not get to Gross Rosen. This was a little out of reach for us. But the whole family had the experience of going through the grounds where our people suffered so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's so much controversy about, you kno, what kind of a man Schindler was, what his motives were. What's your opinion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schindler, from the first day that I met him, when I arrived there as a total youngster, and being, being 15, but nevertheless looking probably like an 11 year old kid, you could almost sense that here is a decent individual, despite the fact that he pointed me out as a youngster. And on reviewing this later, I, I could see it and feel it because I learned so much about him. And you almost felt it during the whole war, that being near him is like being near somebody that has the potential of magic.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I would say the first incident of mine that I had where he pointed me out as a child-- his motives were not dishonorable. His motives were potentially those of looking for strong, healthy workers. Therefore, he didn't want a child. And he also did not the realize that the danger he is putting me together with this other fellow by the name of [INAUDIBLE], who lives in Israel-- there were two kids that he actually-- he actually pointed out. So I, I would have to write off that incident as not describing Oskar Schindler. Oskar Schindler was, from day one, or maybe day two, a warm individual. And you could sense it. Is there anything that you'd like to add that we haven't talked about?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] important thing in our life that played a big role is that arriving in this country, we were given the-- a great opportunity to establish ourselves again as people. And, and I must say that, as a family unit, we have tried our best to participate in, in good things and doing good things, doing good things for others, doing good things for ourselves. And we have many a reason to be extremely grateful to this country. Israel Is not far from our mind. Israel is a country that we must help maintain, that we must be by Israel's side all the time, because what we went through we know that there must be a country someplace which identifies with Jews, and if Jews are in trouble any place in the world, Israel is the country that might have a chance of helping them, because as we learned from World War II, there was simply no one else that was able or willing to take a stand, and stand up, and help the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that your wife has joined you, is there anything either you-- would you like to add or say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Ada, this is your chance. This is my chance.

speaker: interviewee
text: So speak-- so speak up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I will say it, that just recently our kids-- they have-- that is the three children, the grandchildren, and the son-in-law and daughter-in-law-- have thrown a surprise 45th anniversary party for us and a birthday party for me-- 70th birthday. I would say upon reviewing our lives and upon reviewing the notes that are in our memorabilia from this surprise party, I'm reminded that we-- together in the 45 years, we have done many things, many good things. We had many ups and downs in the family and many tragedies we lived through, but we have survived as a unit, strong. We have done for our children what most families would hope to do. We have strived for and achieved their education. We have not only lived for ourselves. We lived for the community. And this is something we can look back with pride on and say, we've done it all.

speaker: interviewee
text: We introduced them to every sport-- to skiing, to ice skating, to golfing, to playing tennis. We did all together with them. They are continuing doing that. We are very proud.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, Sol, you have a book?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I have this book that was actually created-- it's a-- it's a, a little album that was created right upon the liberation from Brinnlitz. And if I may just recall some of these notes, one girl, who is no longer alive, sadly, wrote the following. 'If you forget about me, I hope not, because the common long imprisonment has bound us up in knots of a great friendship for life, which you begin anew. I wish you lots of luck, and above all, you should find your close ones and begin to live again.'

speaker: interviewee
text: We met her in Israel. [INAUDIBLE] But she passed away, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: One fellow writes here-- and this is a fellow inmate that I remember-- that I-- fellow by the name of Chaim Nussbaum. 'Let us rejoice, be happy as loved ones should be. The fresh grass didn't grow yet. Still we are thankful. Our faces are jubilant. My heart, your heart is happy. The beauty of youth, the beauty of happiness is outstanding. They didn't disappear too far. Our whole life we shall reach for love. We shall rejoice and sing in eternal happiness. To my friend in thoughtful times--' No-- 'to my friend in troubled times who was with me in the depth of the dark, Shlomo Urbach.' These are comments that are not made today. These were comments that were made upon liberation just the day after we were liberated. And I have a number of those people-- the people that I have signed in here in my-- in my album are-- a fellow by the name of Olek Allerhand, a girl by the name of [INAUDIBLE], another [INAUDIBLE], a Ferber, [INAUDIBLE], Herman [INAUDIBLE], David, a [INAUDIBLE], of [INAUDIBLE] David, [INAUDIBLE], another [INAUDIBLE], but it was the initial R. [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], and [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], and some names that I cannot recognize anymore. But this is the-- a group of friends that we have formed while we were together in concentration camp on liberation day. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's continue doing the best we can, and, and our family should know of happiness.

speaker: interviewee
text: You going to introduce our family? You have a picture [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we will do that a little later.

words.text: we will do that a little

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll do the pictures in a moment.

words.text: do the pictures in a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: We will do it a little later, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said before, I just lost my father two months ago. Was a wedding in Israel. We had to come back because of the funeral.

speaker: interviewee
text: These were the ups and downs. We have lost Ada's mother, which became a mother to me. We have lost Ada's father. These are the-- we have lost the uncle and aunt who brought me here. We have lost cousins. It's just the ups and downs of life that we went through. But we must look to the future and continue on. We've got good reasons to continue, and to live, and be happy together with our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah? Touchy?
